title: How protector parts react when therapy gets too close
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The moment therapy gets too close

You're sitting in your therapist's office. Or maybe you're reading a book about trauma. Or listening to a podcast that finally names something you've felt for decades. And something shifts. A crack of light enters a room you'd sealed shut. You feel seen. Understood. Maybe even hopeful.

And then something else happens.

Your body goes cold. Your mind goes blank. You start yawning uncontrollably. Or you get suddenly exhausted. Or irritable. Or you find yourself thinking "this is all bullshit" and you're already halfway to the door. You might pick a fight with your therapist. Or you might just stop showing up.

Here's the thing. That's not you being resistant. That's not you being broken. That's not you being "not ready."

That's your protector parts doing their job.

And honestly? They're damn good at it.

I remember the first time a therapist said something that actually landed. I felt a warmth spread through my chest. For about three seconds. Then a voice in my head said "she doesn't know what she's talking about. This is a waste of money. You're fine. Nothing happened. Let's go." I argued with her for the rest of the session. I walked out and almost quit therapy entirely.

That voice was a protector part. And it was terrified.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how the nervous system has its own intelligence. When therapy gets too close to stored trauma, the system doesn't always say "this is too much." Sometimes it says "this therapist is an idiot." Sometimes it says "I'm bored." Sometimes it says "I need to check my email right now."

Richard Schwartz, who developed Internal Family Systems (IFS), calls these protector parts. They're not bad. They're not wrong. They're the parts of you that kept you alive when no one else would. They're the parts that learned to shut down, fight back, or disappear so you could survive.

And when therapy starts to touch the vulnerable parts they've been guarding for years, decades? They don't just step aside politely. They activate. They mobilize. They do what they've always done.

Stay with me here. This matters.

What protectors actually protect

Let's get specific about what's underneath.

When a protector part slams the brakes on therapy, it's not protecting you from the therapist. It's protecting you from the exile. That's what IFS calls the younger parts of you that carry the original pain. The shame. The terror. The abandonment. The belief that something is wrong with you.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. She writes about how we can meet our most rejected parts with compassion instead of more rejection. But here's the catch. Your protector might read that title and say "radical acceptance sounds like giving up." That's the protector talking. That's the part that learned that letting your guard down means getting hurt.

Protectors come in many forms. Some are obvious. The inner critic that tells you you're faking it. The perfectionist that won't let you rest. The people-pleaser that says yes when every cell screams no. Others are subtler. The part that intellectualizes everything, turning trauma into a fascinating case study. The part that spiritual bypasses, saying "everything happens for a reason" before you've even felt the grief. The part that keeps you busy, busy, busy so you never have to sit still with what's inside.

Janina Fisher, who wrote about structural dissociation, describes how these parts developed in response to overwhelming experience. A child who was punished for crying develops a part that says "never show emotion." A child who was ignored develops a part that says "be perfect so someone will see you." A child who was hurt develops a part that says "trust no one."

These parts aren't your enemy. They're your survival team. But they're working with outdated intel. They're still operating as if you're that child in that house, even though you're an adult in a therapist's office with a cup of tea and a box of tissues nearby.

Think about it. When was the last time a protector part took over? Maybe it was last week. Maybe it was five minutes ago while reading this. Notice what just happened in your body. Did your shoulders tighten? Did your chest get heavy? Did your mind start to wander? That's the system doing its job.

The specific ways protectors show up in the room

Let me name some patterns. See if any of them sound familiar.

The skeptic. This part questions everything. The therapist's credentials. The research. The modality. The price. The parking situation. It finds the flaw in every intervention. It's smart and sharp and it's been burned before. It's not wrong. But it's also not letting anything in.

The caretaker. This part worries about the therapist. "She looks tired. I hope I'm not too much. I should ask how she's doing." It's so focused on taking care of others that there's no room for its own needs. This part learned early that your safety depended on managing the adults around you.

The avoider. This part changes the subject. Makes jokes. Forgets appointments. Shows up late. Falls asleep. Gets suddenly hungry. It's the master of the gentle redirect. It doesn't attack therapy. It just makes sure you never quite get there.

The fighter. This part argues. Disagrees. Finds the therapist wrong, naive, or insufficiently educated. It's been hurt by authority figures before and it's not about to let another one in. It would rather push you out of therapy than risk you being hurt again.

The numb one. This part just goes blank. You sit in the chair and there's nothing. No feelings. No thoughts. No memories. It's like someone pulled the plug. This part learned that disconnection was the only way to survive what couldn't be escaped.

I've been all of these. Sometimes in the same session. I've argued with therapists. I've ghosted therapists. I've sat in silence for entire sessions because a part of me was certain that if I spoke, I would shatter into pieces I could never put back together.

Wild, right? That we have whole teams inside us, running strategies we don't even know about.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) by Arielle Schwartz. It gives you concrete exercises for working with these protective patterns. But again, a protector might say "a workbook? That's for beginners. My trauma is too complex for a workbook." Notice that. That's the part keeping you safe by keeping you separate.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) by Nedra Glover Tawwab. Boundaries are often protector-driven at first. "I need space" can be a genuine need, or it can be a protector saying "get away before I get hurt." Both are valid. The work is learning to tell the difference.

What helps when protectors take the wheel

Here's what I've learned. You don't fire your protectors. You don't overpower them. You don't shame them for existing. You thank them.

Literally. Inside your head. "Thank you for keeping me alive. I know you're scared. I'm not going to fire you. I'm just asking you to step back a little so I can see what's underneath."

That's not spiritual bypass. That's neurology. Dan Siegel talks about how naming a state helps integrate it. When you acknowledge the protector, it doesn't have to scream so loud. It can relax a fraction of an inch.

Here are some things that actually help, from someone who's been tackled by protectors more times than I can count.

Slow down. Not "slow down as a concept." Actually slow down. If your protector is revving, your nervous system is in high gear. Breathe slower than you think you need to. Put your hand on your chest. Say "we're not going anywhere. We have time."

Get curious instead of critical. When a protector shows up, don't fight it. Ask it questions. "What are you afraid would happen if I felt that feeling? What's the worst case scenario? How old do you think I am right now?" The answers might surprise you.

Externalize it. Give the protector a name. A shape. A color. I have one I call The Manager. She wears a blazer and carries a clipboard and she's very, very busy. When I feel her taking over, I can say "I see you, Manager. I appreciate you. But I'm going to feel this feeling for five minutes, and then you can have the clipboard back."

Negotiate, don't command. Protectors respond to negotiation. "I know you don't want me to cry in therapy. What if I just tell my therapist that I'm scared to cry? Would that be okay? Can we try that and see what happens?"

Deb Dana, who writes about polyvagal theory, describes how we can work with the nervous system's protective responses rather than against them. She talks about "glimmers" - small moments of safety that help the system renegotiate what's safe. A protector doesn't need to trust your therapist overnight. It just needs to trust that you're not going to throw it under the bus.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. It's the book that helped me understand that even the parts I hated most were trying to help me. Even the ones that kept me stuck. Even the ones that sabotaged my relationships. Even the ones that told me I was worthless.

Especially those ones.

The open ending

I don't have a neat conclusion for this. Because there isn't one.

Your protectors aren't going to disappear. They're not supposed to. They're part of you. The goal isn't to get rid of them. The goal is to have a better relationship with them. To let them know that you're an adult now. That you have resources. That you're not alone. That the danger they're still scanning for might not be there anymore.

But you can't prove that to them by fighting them. You prove it by showing up. By sitting in the discomfort. By saying "I know you're scared. I'm scared too. But let's try this together."

And sometimes they still won't budge. Sometimes you'll leave therapy and call your friend and say "I don't know why I'm so angry. I don't know why I want to quit. I don't know why I can't just let this in."

That's okay. That's not failure. That's the process.

Your protectors have been on duty for a long time. They've earned some trust issues. The fact that they're still showing up means they still care. They still believe you're worth protecting.

And you are.

Even when it's hard. Even when you're fighting yourself. Even when therapy gets too close and everything inside you screams to run.

You're still here. And that's not nothing.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) - A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) - A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate
